paragraph: Korvex Labs, a developer infrastructure startup headquartered in Austin, Texas, announced a $47 million Series B round led by Pelham Ventures, with participation from Aldgate Growth Partners and Thornfield Capital. The company, founded in 2021 by former distributed-systems engineers Maren Voss and Declan Ohara, has built a unified API gateway platform called Vorthex Cloud that allows engineering teams to manage rate limiting, authentication, and traffic shaping across polyglot microservice architectures. Korvex reported annualized recurring revenue of $11.2 million, representing 280 percent year-over-year growth, and said its platform now processes more than 14 billion API calls per month across 620 enterprise customers. The fresh capital will be used to expand the company's European go-to-market team, which currently operates out of Dublin, and to accelerate development of its AI-assisted anomaly detection layer. Pelham Ventures general partner Sylvia Krentz will join the Korvex board alongside existing investor Rupert Almendaz of Thornfield Capital. The company employs 112 people across Austin, Dublin, and a newly opened Singapore hub, and said it expects to reach $30 million in ARR by the end of the fiscal year.

paragraph: Drelm Analytics, a Boston-based financial data intelligence company, closed a $22 million Series A financing round co-led by Brackenfell Partners and Osmund Street Ventures. Founded in 2022 by quant-finance veteran Priya Nattarajan and software architect Tomasz Wierzbicki, Drelm has developed the Mendovian developer platform, which enables hedge funds and asset managers to pipe alternative data sets — including satellite imagery, web-scraping signals, and credit-card transaction feeds — directly into proprietary quantitative models without bespoke data-engineering work. The company said it serves 38 buy-side firms managing a combined $1.4 trillion in assets under management, and that its median contract value has grown from $80,000 to $210,000 over the past 18 months. Drelm's technology reduces the typical data-onboarding cycle from 14 weeks to under four days, according to the company. The Series A proceeds will fund hiring of 30 additional quantitative data engineers and the buildout of a real-time streaming ingestion layer slated for release in the third quarter. Brackenfell managing partner Osei Frimpong will join the Drelm board. The startup employs 54 people and maintains offices in Boston and London's Canary Wharf.

paragraph: Halberg Robotics, a Munich-based industrial automation startup, raised 63 million euros in a Series C round led by Vestholm Growth Fund out of Stockholm, with co-investment from Rheingold Industrial Ventures and a strategic commitment from a major German automotive OEM that declined to be named. The company was founded in 2019 by roboticist Dr. Franziska Drexler and controls engineer Boris Kalmbach, and it produces a line of collaborative robot arms under the Auris Series brand that are purpose-built for unstructured warehouse environments. Unlike conventional industrial robots that require rigid fixturing, Halberg's arms use a proprietary vision-language model to interpret natural-language pick-and-place instructions delivered via a tablet interface, eliminating the need for specialist programming staff. The company said revenue grew 190 percent in the previous fiscal year to reach 18 million euros, and that its current order backlog stands at 31 million euros. The Series C funding will support construction of a second manufacturing facility in Poznan, Poland, expected to triple annual production capacity to 4,200 units per year. Halberg employs 230 people across Munich, Poznan, and a U.S. sales office in Detroit.

paragraph: Solvara Health, a San Francisco-based digital health company focused on chronic kidney disease management, announced a $38 million Series B led by Cairnbrook Life Sciences Fund, with participation from Medvest Capital and Greystone Health Partners. Co-founders Dr. Amara Osei-Bonsu, a nephrologist, and Leila Farahani, a machine learning engineer, launched the company in 2020 to address what they describe as a $90 billion global gap in early-stage CKD monitoring. Solvara's platform combines continuous biomarker tracking via a wearable patch with a predictive model that flags patients at risk of progressing to end-stage renal disease up to 18 months before standard clinical indicators would detect decline. The company currently partners with 47 nephrology practices and 12 regional health systems across the United States, and said it manages care for 94,000 enrolled patients. Solvara's ARR reached $14.7 million at the close of the most recent quarter, up 320 percent from the prior year. The new capital will fund FDA clearance efforts for an upgraded patch sensor and a planned expansion into Canada and the United Kingdom. Cairnbrook partner Dr. Isolde Merriweather will join the board alongside existing director Kwame Asante of Medvest Capital. The company employs 88 people.

paragraph: Terranova AgriSystems, an Ames, Iowa-based precision agriculture startup, closed a $19 million seed extension led by Prairiewind Ventures, joined by Cornerfield Capital and the agricultural investment arm of a Midwestern cooperative lender. Founded by soil scientist Dr. Cole Vanderhoek and embedded-systems engineer Saoirse Brennan in 2021, Terranova has developed a distributed sensor mesh called the FieldGrid Network that monitors soil nitrogen, moisture, pH, and microbial activity at one-meter resolution across commercial row-crop fields. The system uploads telemetry via a low-power LoRaWAN backbone to Terranova's cloud platform, which generates variable-rate input prescription maps that integrate directly with leading precision-ag OEM equipment. Early adopter trials across 280,000 acres in Iowa, Illinois, and Minnesota showed average input-cost reductions of 17 percent and yield improvements of 8 percent compared to conventional management zones. Terranova said it has signed acreage-based SaaS contracts covering 1.1 million acres and is targeting 4 million acres under management by year-end. The funding will be used to manufacture the next generation of sensor nodes and hire 20 agronomists to support grower onboarding. The company currently employs 41 people and plans to open a regional office in Fargo, North Dakota.

paragraph: Crestline Security, a Tel Aviv-founded, New York-headquartered cybersecurity SaaS company, announced a $55 million Series C round co-led by Whitmore Cyber Fund and Northgate Equity Partners. The company, established in 2018 by veteran intelligence analysts Nir Blum and Roni Shapira, offers an adversarial exposure management platform that continuously simulates attacker behavior across an enterprise's hybrid infrastructure, prioritizing vulnerabilities by exploitability rather than severity score alone. Crestline said its platform reduced mean-time-to-remediation by 67 percent for enterprise clients in internal benchmarking. The company now counts 210 enterprise clients in financial services, healthcare, and critical infrastructure, with an average contract value of $340,000. ARR stands at $28.6 million, up 155 percent year over year. Whitmore Cyber Fund managing partner Elspeth Gundry will join the board. The company plans to use the Series C proceeds to launch a managed threat simulation service for mid-market companies priced at $48,000 per year, and to double its U.S. sales headcount to 80 people. Crestline currently employs 195 staff across Tel Aviv, New York, and London, and recently opened a sales development hub in Austin. The company said it expects to surpass $60 million in ARR within 18 months.

paragraph: Lumocycle Energy, a climate tech startup based in Calgary, Canada, raised CAD 85 million in a Series C round led by Verdant Infrastructure Fund and Oakhaven Climate Capital, with participation from a major Canadian pension fund's private equity arm. Founded in 2017 by chemical engineer Dr. Petra Haselberg and energy-markets economist James Okafor, Lumocycle has developed a grid-scale iron-air battery storage system that uses a low-cost, earth-abundant electrochemistry to deliver 100-hour discharge durations at a projected installed cost of $18 per kilowatt-hour. The company completed its first commercial project in October of the prior year, a 40-megawatt installation paired with a wind farm in southern Alberta, and said the system has operated at 99.2 percent availability since commissioning. Lumocycle has signed letters of intent for 620 megawatts of additional projects across Canada, the United States, and Australia. The Series C capital will fund construction of a pilot gigafactory in Calgary capable of producing 200 megawatt-hours of storage cells annually, with the intent of scaling to 2 gigawatt-hours by 2028. The company employs 170 engineers and technicians and said it is in advanced permitting discussions for a second manufacturing site in Ontario.

paragraph: Paxivo, a Singapore-headquartered B2B payments infrastructure startup serving Southeast Asian SMEs, announced a $31 million Series B led by Raffles Hill Ventures, with co-investment from Archipelago Capital and Indus Growth Partners. The company was founded in 2020 by payments veteran Ananda Subramaniam and fintech engineer Mai Linh Tran to solve cross-border invoice settlement delays that average 42 days across the ASEAN corridor. Paxivo's platform connects to local banking rails in eight countries — including Indonesia, Vietnam, Thailand, and the Philippines — and uses real-time FX netting and a pre-funded liquidity pool to settle B2B payments within four hours at a fraction of traditional wire transfer costs. The startup processes $280 million in monthly payment volume, up from $34 million a year prior, and has grown its merchant base to 11,400 active SMEs. Revenue reached an annualized $8.4 million. Paxivo said it plans to use the new capital to obtain payment-service-provider licenses in Malaysia and Cambodia, launch a working-capital lending product backed by receivables data, and grow its engineering team from 65 to 110. Raffles Hill Ventures managing director Claudia Wee will take a board seat alongside existing investor Sanjay Mehrotra of Indus Growth Partners.

paragraph: Velmira, a New York City-based consumer fintech targeting Gen Z savings behavior, raised a $16 million Series A led by Bowery Growth Capital, with participation from Fulcrum Consumer Fund and angel commitments from several fintech executives. Co-founders Jasmine Okafor and Dimitri Stavros launched Velmira in 2022 after research showed that adults aged 18-27 save at a median rate of 2.3 percent of income, compared with 8.1 percent for adults over 40. The Velmira app gamifies savings goals through a behavioral-nudge engine that analyzes discretionary spending patterns and surfaces micro-saving opportunities in real time, rounding up card transactions and automatically routing excess to goal-based sub-accounts. The company has accumulated 340,000 registered users, of whom 110,000 are monthly actives, and said users save an average of $187 more per month after 90 days on the platform than they did before onboarding. Monetization comes through a $4.99 monthly premium subscription and interchange revenue on a co-branded debit card. Annualized revenue stands at $3.1 million. The Series A capital will fund a product expansion into investing features, including fractional ETF purchases, slated for the second half of the year. Bowery Growth Capital partner Renata Vidal will join the Velmira board.

paragraph: Quorra Systems, a semiconductor design startup headquartered in San Jose, California, closed a $72 million Series C round co-led by Redwood Chip Ventures and Cascade Horizon Capital, with strategic participation from a major Asian memory manufacturer. Founded in 2019 by chip architects Dr. Hiroshi Kanda and Prathima Venkatesh, Quorra is designing a family of near-memory compute processors — marketed under the Helix Accelerator brand — that execute tensor operations inside the memory subsystem rather than shuttling data to a separate processing core. The company claims its Helix H2 chip delivers 4.7 times the energy efficiency of incumbent GPU-based approaches for large-batch inference workloads. Quorra taped out its first engineering sample in the prior quarter using a 5-nanometer foundry process and has signed pre-production agreements with two hyperscale cloud operators. The Series C proceeds will fund a full production tape-out, construction of an in-house validation lab, and expansion of the company's software stack team from 18 to 55 engineers. Quorra employs 160 people across San Jose, Taipei, and a design center in Hyderabad opened last year. Redwood Chip Ventures partner Loretta Finch will join the board alongside existing investor Gerhard Moll of Cascade Horizon.

paragraph: Archivolve, a Chicago-based construction tech SaaS company, announced a $24 million Series A round led by Buildhaven Capital Partners, with co-investment from Lakeshore Ventures and Midwest Opportunity Fund. The Series A capital will fund a 25-person expansion of the company's customer success organization, a structured warranty and punch-list management module, and deeper integrations with Building Information Modeling tools. Archivolve currently employs 78 people and plans to open a regional sales office in Dallas.

paragraph: NovaStem Therapeutics, a biotech startup based in Cambridge, Massachusetts, announced a $95 million Series B financing led by Harrogate Life Sciences Fund, with participation from Meridian BioVentures and a sovereign wealth fund's healthcare allocation. paragraph: Fluxtera, a remote equipment monitoring SaaS startup targeting the oil and gas sector, raised $18 million in a Series A round led by Permian Basin Ventures, with co-investment from Roughneck Capital and Hartwell Energy Fund. The Midland, Texas-based company was founded in 2020 by petroleum engineer Gus Delgado and IoT architect Fatima Al-Rashidi to reduce unplanned downtime on artificial lift systems, which the company says costs U.S. operators approximately $14 billion per year. Fluxtera's platform aggregates sensor data from rod pumps, ESPs, and plunger lifts across well sites, applies physics-informed machine learning models to predict mechanical failures up to 72 hours in advance, and triggers automated work-order generation in operator maintenance systems. The company said its platform monitors 18,500 wellheads across 14 U.S. basins and has reduced unplanned downtime for early adopter operators by an average of 41 percent. ARR stands at $5.2 million. Series A proceeds will fund integration partnerships with major field data management vendors, a wireline sensor hardware program, and the addition of 22 field application engineers. Fluxtera currently employs 49 people and plans to open an office in Calgary to serve Canadian oil sands operators.

paragraph: Groven AI, a Paris-based enterprise AI workflow automation company, announced a 42 million euro Series B led by Quartier Capital and co-led by Nordic Scale Ventures, with participation from Eurotech Growth Partners. Founded in 2021 by former enterprise software executives Mathilde Chabert and Viktor Horak, Groven has built an AI orchestration platform that allows non-technical business analysts to design and deploy document-processing and decision-automation workflows through a visual interface, without writing code. The platform uses a proprietary multi-agent execution runtime that decomposes complex multi-step document tasks — such as contract abstraction, invoice exception handling, and compliance-report assembly — into parallelized subtasks distributed across specialized AI agents. Groven said it has grown ARR to 14.3 million euros over 24 months of general availability, counting 185 enterprise clients across financial services, insurance, and professional services verticals. Net revenue retention stands at 138 percent. The Series B capital will fund a go-to-market push into Germany and the United Kingdom, the launch of a self-service tier for mid-market buyers, and the expansion of Groven's model governance and auditability features. The company currently employs 140 people and is planning to hire 60 additional staff in the next 12 months.

paragraph: Borealis Fleet, a Vancouver-based fleet electrification infrastructure company, closed a CAD 54 million Series B round led by Pacific Greenfield Fund, with co-investment from Northern Cleantech Capital and a major Canadian utility's venture arm. Founded in 2019 by electrical engineer Dr. Ingrid Solberg and logistics-tech entrepreneur Marcus Chen, Borealis Fleet provides turnkey depot charging infrastructure and energy management software for commercial fleet operators transitioning to battery-electric vehicles. The company's platform dynamically manages charging loads across depot sites to minimize peak demand charges, integrates with fleet telematics to schedule vehicles for charging based on dispatch requirements, and provides utilities with demand-response aggregation signals. Borealis said it has deployed infrastructure supporting 1,200 electric commercial vehicles across 38 fleet depot sites, primarily serving transit agencies, last-mile delivery operators, and municipal governments. Revenue grew 210 percent year over year to CAD 12.4 million. The new capital will fund expansion into the U.S. Pacific Northwest market, a battery-second-life storage partnership, and the buildout of a federal procurement compliance team. Borealis currently employs 95 people across Vancouver and a newly opened Seattle office.

paragraph: Syndex, a San Francisco-based legal operations platform for in-house corporate legal teams, raised a $29 million Series B led by Cornerstone Venture Partners, with participation from LegalEdge Capital and Fulcrum B2B Fund. Founded in 2020 by former BigLaw litigator Camille Bourgeois and enterprise SaaS executive Raj Varma, Syndex offers a matter management, legal spend analytics, and outside-counsel billing auditing platform that plugs into existing law department ERP and HRIS systems. The company said customers using its AI-assisted invoice review module reduce outside counsel spend by an average of 14 percent in the first year, through automated UTBMS code compliance checking and timekeeper rate benchmarking against a proprietary billing database covering 280 law firms. Syndex reported $9.1 million in ARR, up 245 percent from the prior year, and said its net revenue retention rate is 129 percent. The company serves 94 in-house legal departments at Fortune-equivalent companies, with a median contract value of $96,000. Series B proceeds will fund the launch of a litigation analytics module, the expansion of the company's implementation services team, and new integrations with e-discovery platforms. Syndex currently employs 83 people in San Francisco and New York.

paragraph: Terrament Labs, a deep-tech startup based in Zurich, announced a 38 million Swiss franc seed financing round — an unusually large seed for a hardware company — led by AlpineX Deep Tech Fund and co-led by Helion Frontier Capital. The company was founded in 2022 by geophysicist Dr. Reto Bischoff and mechanical engineer Dr. Yasmin Farsi to commercialize a novel underground thermal battery concept that stores gigawatt-hours of energy as heat in engineered subsurface rock formations. Terrament's approach requires no rare-earth materials, uses low-cost drilling technology adapted from the geothermal industry, and is designed for a 50-year operational lifespan. The company said laboratory-scale experiments have validated the core thermal charge-discharge cycle with 89 percent round-trip efficiency. The seed financing will fund construction of a pilot system rated at 10 megawatt-hours in a disused mine in the Swiss canton of Graubunden, targeted for completion within 22 months. AlpineX Deep Tech Fund partner Dr. Joachim Ritter will join the Terrament board. The company plans to use the pilot results to anchor a Series A round in the range of 80 to 120 million Swiss francs. Terrament currently employs 24 engineers and scientists.

paragraph: Meriton Health, a Nashville-based value-based care enablement company, closed a $43 million Series C led by Broadwater Health Ventures, with co-investment from Avalon Medical Fund and a strategic investment from a regional Blue Cross Blue Shield affiliate. Founded in 2018 by Dr. Philip Okonkwo and health economist Meredith Cassidy, Meriton provides a clinical data aggregation and risk-stratification platform that helps independent primary care practices transition from fee-for-service to value-based reimbursement contracts. The platform ingests claims, lab, prescription, and social determinants data from 17 disparate source systems, surfacing high-risk patients and gap-in-care alerts within the clinician's existing EHR workflow. Meriton said it supports 1,100 primary care physicians managing 480,000 attributed lives under Medicare Advantage and commercial value-based contracts, and that practices on its platform achieve an average quality bonus uplift of $34,000 per physician per year. ARR reached $17.6 million at the most recent quarter end. The Series C proceeds will fund expansion into new value-based contract types, including specialty care bundled payments, and the launch of a patient engagement mobile app. Meriton employs 128 people and operates from Nashville with a secondary office in Baltimore.

paragraph: Candela Commerce, a Toronto-based e-commerce infrastructure startup, announced a $21 million Series A round led by Harbourfront Ventures, with participation from Crosstown Growth Capital and several family offices with retail industry backgrounds. paragraph: Plexion Systems, a Detroit-based industrial IoT platform company focused on predictive maintenance for discrete manufacturers, raised a $33 million Series B led by Riverton Industrial Fund, with co-investment from Ironworks Equity Partners and a Tier 1 automotive supplier's corporate venture arm. The system's anomaly detection models — trained on Plexion's proprietary fault-signature library covering 9,400 equipment types — flag potential failures and recommend maintenance interventions with an average lead time of 11 days. The company said its platform monitors 68,000 assets across 312 manufacturing facilities in North America, Europe, and Japan, and that customers experience an average reduction in unplanned downtime of 38 percent in the first year of deployment. Series B proceeds will fund the expansion of Plexion's European operations from a Munich sales office to a full regional hub, and the launch of an automated parts-procurement integration with industrial distributors. The company employs 148 people.

paragraph: Callio Bioanalytics, a San Diego-based life sciences tools startup, announced a $26 million Series A led by Biocoast Ventures, with participation from Scalpel Capital and Pacific Rim Life Sciences Fund. Founded in 2021 by Dr. Anya Kowalczyk and Dr. Emeka Eze, Callio has developed a benchtop multi-omic profiling instrument — the Prism 500 — that simultaneously measures gene expression, protein abundance, and metabolite levels from the same single-cell sample in under four hours, a workflow that currently requires three separate instruments and up to three days in leading academic labs. The Prism 500 uses a patented microfluidic chip architecture and a custom spectral analysis engine to achieve what the company describes as the highest single-cell multi-modal information density commercially available. Callio said it has shipped 42 instruments to academic medical centers and biopharma drug discovery groups, generating $4.7 million in instrument and reagent revenue over the prior 12 months. The Series A capital will fund a manufacturing scale-up, the hiring of 15 field application scientists, and the development of a cloud-based data analysis platform that will launch as a subscription service priced at $18,000 per year per lab. The company employs 68 people in San Diego.

paragraph: Hestian, a London-based climate risk intelligence SaaS company, raised 31 million pounds in a Series B led by Greengate Impact Capital, with co-investment from Aldgate Growth Partners and a European development finance institution. Founded in 2020 by climatologist Dr. Sophie Beaumont and insurance actuary James Osei, Hestian provides a platform that helps banks, insurers, and real estate asset managers quantify climate-related financial risks — physical hazards such as flood and wildfire as well as transition risks from carbon pricing policy — at the individual asset and portfolio level. The platform integrates global climate model outputs with proprietary geospatial analytics and regulatory scenario frameworks including TCFD, SFDR, and the Bank of England's Climate Biennial Exploratory Scenario. Hestian said it serves 68 institutional clients overseeing a combined 8.7 trillion pounds in assets, and that its platform has mapped climate exposures for 2.3 million distinct property and infrastructure assets. ARR reached 9.4 million pounds, up 265 percent year over year. The Series B proceeds will fund product expansion into nature-related financial disclosures aligned with TNFD frameworks, a U.S. market entry led by a New York office opening, and the doubling of the engineering team. Hestian currently employs 95 people.

paragraph: Veltris, an Austin-based network security startup focused on industrial control system environments, announced a $41 million Series C led by Stonegate Cyber Capital, with participation from Whitmore Cyber Fund and the venture arm of a major defense contractor. Co-founders Nadine Ostrowski and Cyrus Pahlavani launched Veltris in 2018 after careers in critical infrastructure protection at a national cybersecurity agency. The company's platform uses passive network traffic analysis to build behavioral baselines of industrial control system devices — PLCs, RTUs, HMIs, and historians — and alerts operations technology security teams to deviations that could indicate intrusion or sabotage. Unlike agent-based solutions, Veltris requires no software installation on operational technology assets, making it safe to deploy in environments where patch cycles are measured in years rather than weeks. The company serves 115 clients across energy, water, and manufacturing sectors, with an average annual contract value of $280,000. ARR reached $22.4 million, representing 140 percent year-over-year growth. The Series C capital will fund expansion of Veltris's threat intelligence team, the development of a physical security convergence module, and certification for deployment in U.S. federal critical infrastructure environments. The company employs 162 people across Austin and a Washington, D.C., office.

paragraph: Norval Education, a New York City-based edtech company targeting corporate learning and development for frontline workers, raised a $17 million Series A led by Workforce Innovation Fund, with co-investment from Harborview Venture Partners. The company serves 63 enterprise clients and manages learning programs for 340,000 enrolled learners. ARR stands at $5.8 million. Series A proceeds will fund the development of an AI-assisted content authoring tool that lets L&D teams generate localized training modules in 22 languages, and the expansion of Norval's direct sales team from 11 to 30 representatives. The company currently employs 57 people.

paragraph: Perova Materials, a Pittsburgh-based advanced materials startup, announced a $52 million Series B led by Deeptech Horizon Partners, with participation from Carnegie Innovation Fund and a major specialty chemicals corporation's corporate venture arm. Founded by materials scientist Dr. Lena Wojcik and electrochemist Dr. Tariq Hassan in 2019, Perova has developed a process for manufacturing solid-state battery electrolyte films at scale using a continuous roll-to-roll deposition technique adapted from thin-film solar manufacturing. The company's electrolyte material — a proprietary lithium-ceramic composite — demonstrates ionic conductivity above 3 milliSiemens per centimeter and operates stably from negative 40 to positive 120 degrees Celsius, expanding the viable operating range for next-generation automotive batteries. Perova said it has signed joint development agreements with two electric vehicle manufacturers and one battery cell supplier, and that its pilot production line in Pittsburgh is producing 8,000 square meters of electrolyte film per month. The Series B proceeds will fund a ten-fold scale-up of film production capacity, the construction of a materials characterization center, and the filing of 34 additional patent applications. Perova employs 89 scientists and engineers, with plans to grow to 150 by year-end.

paragraph: Glyph Data, a Seattle-based data observability and lineage platform for modern data stacks, closed a $28 million Series B round led by Highgate Venture Growth, with participation from Steller Data Ventures and Crossover Growth Capital. paragraph: Luminary Freight, a Miami-based freight technology startup serving Latin American cross-border trade lanes, announced a $23 million Series A led by Brickell Growth Partners, with co-investment from Palmas Capital and Caribbean Horizon Ventures. paragraph: Arentis, a Stockholm-based HR tech startup specializing in workforce planning and organizational design for large enterprises, announced a 26 million euro Series B led by Scandis Growth Fund, with participation from Nordea Ventures and Albatross Capital. Founded in 2019 by organizational psychologist Dr. Sigrid Holm and enterprise software architect Johan Petersson, Arentis provides a continuous workforce planning platform that models headcount scenarios, maps role-to-skill requirements, and generates restructuring impact analyses in real time — a workflow that companies currently accomplish through quarterly spreadsheet exercises managed by finance and HR in parallel. The platform integrates with HRIS, ERP, and finance planning systems via a unified data model and provides scenario comparison tools that allow CHROs and CFOs to collaborate on org design decisions with a shared data layer. Arentis said it serves 82 enterprise clients with an average workforce size of 14,000 employees, and that customers reduce annual workforce planning cycle time by an average of 65 percent. ARR stands at 8.7 million euros. The Series B capital will fund a U.K. market launch, the development of an AI-assisted skills gap analysis module, and the expansion of the customer success team from 12 to 30 people. Arentis employs 94 people across Stockholm and London.

paragraph: Tandem Supply, a Chicago-based B2B e-procurement platform for industrial distributors and their manufacturing customers, raised a $37 million Series C led by Midpoint Equity Partners, with co-investment from Gatehouse Industrial Fund and Loop Capital Ventures. Founded in 2018 by supply chain consultant Nadia Brinkman and marketplace entrepreneur Patrick Yuen, Tandem Supply operates as a vertical marketplace connecting 1,200 stocking distributors of MRO, safety, and indirect materials with 4,800 manufacturing plant procurement teams. The platform offers catalog aggregation, multi-distributor punch-out ordering, electronic invoicing, and spend analytics through a single interface, reducing the average procurement cycle for indirect materials from 4.2 days to 6.8 hours. Tandem processes $740 million in annualized gross merchandise value and takes a 1.8 percent take rate on transactions, generating approximately $13.3 million in annualized revenue. Net revenue retention is 141 percent. The Series C proceeds will fund the launch of a contract and pricing management module, expansion into the Canadian market, and the development of a vendor-managed inventory program for high-velocity MRO items. Tandem currently employs 102 people across Chicago, Cincinnati, and a newly opened Houston distribution intelligence center.

paragraph: Spectra AI, a Singapore-based conversational AI company targeting insurance claims automation in the Asia-Pacific region, announced a $20 million Series A led by Raffles Hill Ventures, with participation from Monsoon Capital and APAC Fintech Fund. Founded in 2021 by former insurance technology executives Ling Wei Chen and Divya Krishnan, Spectra has built an end-to-end claims intake and triage platform that allows policyholders to file and track claims via a multilingual conversational interface supporting English, Mandarin, Bahasa Indonesia, and Thai. The system uses a proprietary claims classification model trained on 8.4 million historical claim documents to automatically route submissions, request supporting documents, and generate coverage eligibility determinations for 87 percent of simple claims without human adjuster intervention. Spectra said it has partnered with 19 insurers across Singapore, Indonesia, Malaysia, and Thailand, and that the platform processes 140,000 claims per month. Participating insurers report a 58 percent reduction in cost-per-claim for automated determinations. ARR reached $4.6 million. Series A funds will be used to develop a fraud pattern detection module, expand language support to include Vietnamese and Tagalog, and hire 15 additional client integration engineers. The company employs 54 people in Singapore and Kuala Lumpur.

paragraph: Vaudra, a Denver-based SaaS startup serving community banks and credit unions, closed a $15 million seed round co-led by Centennial Financial Tech Fund and Rockies Venture Partners. Founded in 2022 by former community bank executive Thomas Grabowski and fintech engineer Adaeze Nwosu, Vaudra provides a deposit growth and member acquisition platform that gives smaller financial institutions access to digital marketing analytics and AI-driven product personalization capabilities that are typically available only to the largest national banks. The platform integrates with core banking systems from Fiserv-compatible and Jack Henry-equivalent vendors and uses behavioral segmentation to recommend tailored product offers — certificates of deposit, HELOC products, and small business checking accounts — to existing customers at the right moment in their financial journey. The company said it serves 48 community banks and credit unions with a combined $28 billion in assets, and that customers see an average 22 percent increase in targeted product adoption within 90 days. The seed capital will fund the development of a small business loan origination module and the expansion of Vaudra's sales team from 4 to 18 people. The company currently employs 27 people. Centennial Financial Tech Fund partner Allison Schreiber will join the Vaudra board.

paragraph: Olaris Climate, a Rotterdam-based maritime decarbonization startup, raised 49 million euros in a Series B led by Nordsea Climate Fund, with co-investment from Greengate Impact Capital and a major Scandinavian shipping line's corporate venture arm. Founded in 2019 by naval architect Dr. Marius Bakke and energy systems engineer Dr. Yael Shalev, Olaris has developed a wind-assisted propulsion system — the SailFin Array — that uses rigid automated wingsails mounted on cargo vessels to capture wind energy and reduce main engine load. The system is designed for retrofit installation on existing vessels and uses a weather routing AI that predicts optimal sail configurations based on ocean wind data, sea state, and voyage schedule constraints. Olaris said 14 vessels have been equipped with the SailFin Array, generating an average fuel consumption reduction of 18 percent across operational voyages, and that a 1.2 gigawatt-hour reduction in vessel CO2 emissions has been recorded across the installed fleet. The company has a signed order backlog of 38 vessels for installation over the next 24 months. The Series B proceeds will fund a Rotterdam manufacturing scale-up, an engineering center in Oslo, and the development of a next-generation wingsail design with 40 percent greater aerodynamic efficiency. Olaris employs 118 people.

paragraph: Thornwick Labs, a New York-based cybersecurity startup focused on software supply chain security, announced a $35 million Series B led by Crestview Cyber Partners, with co-investment from Stonegate Cyber Capital and Ironclad Ventures. Founded in 2020 by security researcher Petra Vanhoff and software engineer Daisuke Yamamoto, Thornwick provides a software composition analysis and dependency risk intelligence platform that gives AppSec teams continuous visibility into open-source libraries embedded in their code, container images, and CI/CD pipelines. Unlike traditional SCA tools that rely on static vulnerability databases, Thornwick's platform uses dynamic reachability analysis to determine whether a vulnerable code path is actually callable in a given application's runtime, eliminating more than 80 percent of false-positive alerts, according to the company's benchmarks. Thornwick reported ARR of $8.9 million, up 230 percent year over year, and said its platform monitors 120,000 software repositories for 480 enterprise and mid-market customers. Net revenue retention is 131 percent. Series B proceeds will fund the development of a secrets detection module, a software bill of materials management feature aligned with emerging regulatory requirements, and the expansion of the go-to-market team. Thornwick employs 78 people in New York and a remote-first engineering organization.

paragraph: Zolara, an Abuja-based fintech startup serving informal-economy micro-merchants in West Africa, announced a $12 million Series A round led by Savanna Ventures, with participation from Lagos Angel Network and East-West Emerging Markets Fund. Founded in 2021 by payments entrepreneur Chidi Eze and data scientist Amina Sani, Zolara provides a smartphone-based point-of-sale and business management tool tailored for the roughly 40 million micro-retailers across Nigeria, Ghana, and Senegal who operate without formal banking relationships or business registration. The platform allows merchants to accept QR-code payments via mobile money, track inventory and daily cash flow, and access working capital advances of between $50 and $2,000 based on transaction history rather than credit bureau data. Zolara said it has onboarded 180,000 active merchants and that the platform processes $47 million in monthly payment volume. Revenue, generated through payment take rates and lending interest income, reached an annualized $2.8 million. The Series A capital will fund the launch of a merchant insurance product, regulatory applications for payment service licenses in Ghana and Senegal, and the development of a group savings feature. Zolara employs 62 people across Abuja and Lagos, with plans to open a Dakar office in the coming year.

paragraph: Vantrel Systems, a San Jose-based embedded silicon IP licensing company, closed a $58 million Series D led by Cascade Horizon Capital, with participation from Quorum Semiconductor Ventures and a major RISC-V processor ecosystem vendor. Founded in 2016 by chip designer Dr. Meena Raghunathan and EDA software entrepreneur Sebastien Fournier, Vantrel has developed a portfolio of silicon IP blocks for AI inference, cryptographic acceleration, and high-speed SerDes interconnects that chip designers license and integrate into their custom ASIC designs. The company's AI inference engine IP — the NeuralCore Accelerator subsystem — has been licensed into 28 production chip programs across consumer electronics, automotive ADAS, and edge computing applications. Vantrel reported $31.4 million in annualized royalty and licensing revenue, up 88 percent from the prior fiscal year. The Series D funding will be used to double the company's IP development headcount from 55 to 110 engineers, expand the NeuralCore product line with a next-generation block offering eight times the compute density, and open a design center in Bangalore to serve India-based fabless semiconductor clients. Cascade Horizon Capital partner Hiroshi Yamamoto will join the Vantrel board. The company employs 170 people across San Jose, Paris, and Tokyo.

paragraph: Reiform, a Boston-based regulatory technology company serving the pharmaceutical industry, raised a $27 million Series B led by BioVenture Growth Partners, with co-investment from Lifepath Capital and Helix Health Capital. paragraph: Triflex Biologics, a Cambridge, UK-based synthetic biology startup, raised 34 million pounds in a Series B led by Cavendish Life Sciences Fund, with participation from Harrogate Life Sciences Fund and a major contract development and manufacturing organization's strategic investment arm. Cargopath employs 88 people in Los Angeles and Chicago.

paragraph: Cloudpass Security, a Toronto-based cloud access management startup, raised a $22 million Series A led by Northgate Equity Partners, with participation from Crestview Cyber Partners and Harbourfront Ventures. Founded in 2021 by identity security veterans Adeola Ogunwale and Dmitri Ivlev, Cloudpass provides an AI-assisted cloud entitlement management and access governance platform that gives security teams continuous visibility into who — human identities and machine service accounts alike — has access to what in multi-cloud AWS-equivalent, Azure-analogue, and GCP-analogue environments. The platform automatically detects excessive privilege, dormant accounts, and policy deviations, and generates remediation playbooks that can be executed with one click or through automated workflows triggered by ITSM ticketing integrations. Cloudpass said it has 140 paying customers with an average annual contract value of $58,000, and that customers reduce their cloud entitlement attack surface by an average of 74 percent within 90 days of deployment. ARR stands at $6.4 million. The Series A proceeds will fund the development of a just-in-time access provisioning module, deeper integrations with CI/CD pipelines for DevSecOps workflows, and expansion of the sales team from 8 to 22 people. Northgate Equity Partners partner Callum Forsythe will join the Cloudpass board. The company employs 51 people.

paragraph: Litora Aquaculture, a Bergen, Norway-based aquatech startup, announced a $28 million Series B led by Nordsea Climate Fund, with participation from BlueOcean Ventures and a major Norwegian salmon farming cooperative's investment arm. Founded in 2018 by marine biologist Dr. Astrid Engvik and environmental engineer Kjetil Moe, Litora has developed a recirculating aquaculture system — the AquaFlow RAS 2000 — designed for land-based Atlantic salmon farming at commercial scale, reducing the disease risk, sea lice pressure, and water quality issues that afflict open-net pen aquaculture. The system uses a proprietary biofilter stack, UV disinfection, and continuous dissolved-oxygen management to maintain optimal water quality conditions across tanks holding up to 200 metric tons of salmon biomass. Litora said its first commercial installation in Hordaland has operated for 18 months with 99.1 percent fish survival rate and feed conversion ratios 14 percent better than industry averages. The company has signed agreements with three additional operators to install RAS 2000 systems in Norway, the United Kingdom, and Canada. Series B proceeds will fund the manufacturing capacity expansion, regulatory consulting for Canadian certification, and the development of a real-time water chemistry AI monitoring dashboard. Litora employs 63 people.

paragraph: Rendex, a New York-based digital content rights management and licensing platform, raised a $16 million Series A led by Media Horizon Fund, with participation from Fulcrum Consumer Fund and several music industry executives participating as angel investors. Founded in 2021 by music rights attorney Simone Fabiano and software architect Kofi Asante, Rendex provides a SaaS platform that helps independent music publishers, film studios, and sports media rights holders catalog, license, and track commercial usage of their content catalogs programmatically. The platform integrates with global content identification systems, digital service provider reporting feeds, and sync licensing databases to give rights holders real-time visibility into where their content is being used, automate royalty calculations across multiple revenue streams, and generate digital licensing agreements via a templated workflow. Rendex said it manages rights catalogs containing 4.3 million works for 280 client organizations and that it has processed 1.1 million licensing transactions over the past 12 months. ARR stands at $4.8 million. Series A proceeds will fund the development of a blockchain-based rights ownership attestation module, expansion into the sports broadcast rights market, and the addition of 18 engineering and business development staff. The company currently employs 41 people.

paragraph: Brenta Systems, a Raleigh-based IT service management automation startup targeting the managed service provider market, closed a $24 million Series B led by Eastgate SaaS Growth Fund, with co-investment from Southeast Venture Partners and Rockford Capital. Founded in 2019 by MSP industry veterans Derek Thornton and Lisa Huang, Brenta provides a workflow automation and intelligent alert triage platform that sits above the PSA and RMM tools that MSPs use to manage client endpoints. The platform's AI triage engine ingests alerts from 12 integrated RMM platforms and uses historical resolution data from the MSP's own ticket history to automatically classify, prioritize, and route alerts to the appropriate tier of technical staff, suppressing noise that the engine predicts will self-resolve. Brenta said its platform reduces alert noise by an average of 71 percent and cuts mean-time-to-resolution for genuine incidents by 44 percent across its customer base. The company serves 195 MSPs managing a combined 1.8 million endpoints, and reported ARR of $7.6 million, up 220 percent year over year. Series B capital will fund the development of a client-facing reporting portal, a proactive maintenance automation module, and an international expansion starting with Australia and the United Kingdom. Brenta currently employs 68 people.

paragraph: Cadenza Pharma, a Lausanne-based drug discovery startup, announced a 56 million Swiss franc Series C led by BioAlps Investment Fund, with participation from Cavendish Life Sciences Fund and a major Japanese pharmaceutical company's venture unit. Founded by computational chemist Dr. Nadia Pellegrini and structural biologist Dr. Leon Kaufmann in 2017, Cadenza has built a structure-based drug design platform that combines cryo-electron microscopy derived protein structures with a proprietary molecular dynamics simulation engine to accelerate hit-to-lead optimization for difficult-to-drug protein targets. The company's lead oncology program, CAD-301, a selective degrader of a transcription factor implicated in aggressive forms of triple-negative breast cancer, has advanced to Phase I clinical trials at six sites in Switzerland, France, and Germany. Preliminary Phase I data showed tolerability across three dose cohorts with no dose-limiting toxicities and early evidence of target engagement at the two highest doses tested. The Series C proceeds will fund the completion of Phase I dose escalation, the initiation of a Phase Ib expansion cohort, and the advancement of Cadenza's second program, CAD-418, targeting a G protein-coupled receptor in pancreatic cancer. The company employs 92 scientists and clinical development specialists.

paragraph: Strobe Logistics, an Amsterdam-based autonomous last-mile delivery robotics company, raised 39 million euros in a Series B led by Eurotech Growth Partners, with co-investment from Portside Logistics Fund and the mobility investment arm of a major European postal operator. Founded in 2019 by roboticist Dr. Tariq Baroudi and autonomous systems engineer Elise Bergmann, Strobe has developed a six-wheeled sidewalk delivery robot — the Strobe Rover SR-3 — that navigates urban environments at speeds up to 6 kilometers per hour, carries a 15-kilogram payload, and operates for 10 hours on a single battery charge. The SR-3 uses a sensor suite combining lidar, stereo cameras, and ultrasonic sensors, and is powered by an onboard neural network trained on 280 million kilometers of simulated and real-world urban traversal data. Strobe has deployed 840 Rover units across Rotterdam, Brussels, Copenhagen, and Zurich under commercial partnerships with three major grocery chains and a pharmacy network. The company said Rovers completed 1.2 million deliveries in the prior 12 months with a 98.7 percent successful delivery rate. Series B proceeds will fund expansion of the fleet to 3,000 units, a rollout into London pending regulatory approval, and the development of a next-generation Rover platform with 40-kilogram payload capacity. Strobe employs 145 people.

paragraph: Vivara Home, a Los Angeles-based consumer proptech company, announced a $14 million Series A led by Westcoast Growth Capital, with participation from Consumer Frontier Fund and Hearthstone Ventures. Founded in 2022 by real estate data scientist Jade Morales and product designer Felix Okonkwo, Vivara provides a personalized home search and financing pre-qualification app that uses behavioral preference signals gathered during the browsing experience — including time-on-listing, saved amenity clusters, and commute-weight sliders — to build a latent preference model for each user and surface listings ranked by predicted match quality rather than recency or price. The platform integrates with 38 regional MLS data feeds covering 14 U.S. metropolitan areas, presents listings alongside a Vivara Match Score and an automated mortgage pre-qualification estimate powered by a soft-credit-pull API, and surfaces estimated total cost of ownership including property tax, HOA, and insurance projections. Vivara said it has 280,000 registered users, of whom 58,000 are active monthly, and that users who engage with the Match Score ranking spend 40 percent less time searching before submitting an offer. The Series A funds will be used to launch a buyer-agent referral marketplace and expand coverage to 22 additional metropolitan markets. Vivara employs 34 people.

paragraph: Avalis Networks, a Chicago-based telecommunications infrastructure startup, closed a $67 million Series D led by Midpoint Equity Partners and Greyrock Infrastructure Fund, with participation from Crestline Capital and a strategic commitment from a Tier 2 regional wireless carrier. Founded in 2016 by network engineer Brendan Callahan and RF systems architect Dr. Priya Suresh, Avalis has developed a software-defined radio access network platform — the Spectrion Fabric — that virtualizes 5G baseband and radio unit functions to run on commodity server hardware deployed in distributed edge locations, reducing the capital cost of deploying dense urban 5G networks by 55 percent compared to proprietary RAN equipment, according to the company's internal cost models. The Spectrion Fabric is currently deployed across 4,800 radio sites serving two carrier customers in the United States and one in the United Kingdom, supporting 3.1 million end users. Avalis reported ARR of $42 million, up 98 percent from the prior fiscal year, and said it has a contracted backlog of $180 million in software and services revenue. The Series D proceeds will fund a buildout of the company's systems integration team to 150 professionals, the porting of the Spectrion Fabric to additional processor architectures including ARM-based server silicon, and the pursuit of network equipment certification in Japan and South Korea. Avalis employs 320 people across Chicago, London, and Tokyo.
